Industry Context — Systemic vs Organic
Events that moved all Big Four this quarter. When the whole sector moves together, it signals a regulatory or macro driver — not a CBA-specific issue. Separate these from CBA’s own comms narrative.
Systemic Events — Q2 2026 (Illustrative)
Event Type All Banks? Est. Sector Impact

Est. sector impact illustrative. Systemic events affect all banks — CBA-specific score movement above/below this baseline is driven by own comms activity.

💡 When the whole sector moves — don’t panic
If CBA’s score declines in the next RepTrack wave, check whether peers declined equally. Sector-wide drops driven by regulation (e.g. APRA, AFCA) or macro Zeitgeist (e.g. cost-of-living framing) are institutional — they require industry-level response strategy, not a CBA-specific comms fix.

Q2 2026 Outlook: Leadership (▲), Performance (▲), Innovation (▲) signals are strongly positive and trending up. Workplace (▼) under pressure from ongoing industrial relations coverage — recommend proactive narrative ahead of next survey wave. Based on current signals, overall RepTrack score Q2 2026 likely range: 70–73.

Forecast is illustrative. Based on 12-month regression model (n=12). Confidence improves with more data.
Q1 2026 Reputation Scorecard — Jan–Apr 2026  ·  Comms Attribution & RepTrack Performance

What moved the score in Q1 2026

CBA’s overall RepTrack score improved from 66.6 in November 2025 to 69.4 in April 2026 — a gain of 2.8 points over the quarter. An estimated 62% of this movement is attributable to total tracked media signals (both comms-induced and ambient coverage) — the Media Attribution Score. Of all coverage received, 38% was directly triggered by CBA’s own comms activity — the Induction Quotient. These are complementary metrics: one measures score impact, the other measures comms-caused volume. Leadership was the strongest contributor, rising 3.6 points (71.7 → 75.7), driven by CEO Comyn’s AI thought leadership positioning across 3 major press releases and 87 Tier-1 pickups. Conduct improved 4.5 points from its trough (64.0 in Nov-25 to 68.5 in Apr-26), with the proactive call-centre reinstatement announcement correlating to a measurable positive inflection. Performance remained consistently strong (80.6–82.7), contributing stable positive sentiment. Workplace was the quarter’s vulnerability — the WGEA gender pay gap story (ambient, not comms-caused) contributed an estimated −1.2 points pressure.

“
Our AI innovation positioning campaign contributed an estimated +1.8 points to the Leadership pillar in Q1 2026.

“
The proactive call-centre reinstatement announcement measurably improved Conduct sentiment — correlating with a +4.5 point Conduct pillar recovery.
Conduct had been in decline since November 2025. The announcement shifted net sentiment from −4pp to +12pp in 30 days. Recovery traced across Conduct and Workplace pillars.
“
62% of our overall score movement in Q1 2026 is explainable by tracked media and comms activity.

Peer Scorecards — Big Four
Same methodology applied to ANZ, NAB & Westpac · RepTrack scores: real · Attribution: illustrative
ANZ
Nov-25 → Apr-26  ·  Overall: 60.0 → 69.5 ▲ +9.5 pts

What moved ANZ's score in Q1 2026

ANZ delivered the strongest overall score recovery among the Big Four — rising 9.5 points from a November 2025 low of 60.0 to 69.5 in April 2026. The Nov-25 trough coincided with regulatory scrutiny over the Suncorp integration and residual remediation headlines. The recovery was broad-based: Leadership jumped 8.3 points (66.0 → 74.3) as executive messaging on strategic direction gained traction, and Workplace surged 7.8 points (65.4 → 73.2), correlating with improved coverage of ANZ's cultural renewal program. An estimated 55% of the overall recovery is attributable to tracked media and comms activity.

NAB
Nov-25 → Apr-26  ·  Overall: 68.0 → 71.8 ▲ +3.8 pts

What moved NAB's score in Q1 2026

NAB achieved the highest absolute RepTrack score among the Big Four in April 2026 at 71.8, up 3.8 points from November 2025. NAB's Innovation pillar showed the strongest momentum, rising 6.5 points (66.5 → 73.0), driven by the UBank rebranding and digital-first positioning. Products & Services also led the peer group at 75.5, up 5.6 points, reflecting sustained positive coverage of NAB's mortgage rate competitiveness. Leadership gained 5.2 points to reach 76.3 — the highest Leadership score in the Big Four cohort. An estimated 48% of quarterly improvement is attributable to tracked comms activity.

Westpac
Nov-25 → Apr-26  ·  Overall: 68.9 → 68.8 ▶ −0.1 pts (flat)

What moved Westpac's score in Q1 2026

Westpac's overall RepTrack score was essentially flat across the quarter — 68.9 in November 2025 vs 68.8 in April 2026. This conceals a mixed pillar picture: Workplace improved 3.5 points (70.2 → 73.7) and Citizenship gained 3.0 points, supported by comms around Westpac's Social Impact Fund. However, these gains were offset by ambient macro coverage of mortgage arrears and net interest margin pressure, which weighed on the Performance pillar (+0.7 pts, modest). Conduct improved a marginal 0.7 points, signalling ongoing sensitivity to regulatory and customer complaint narratives. An estimated 35% of pillar movement is attributable to tracked media activity — the lowest attribution rate in the Big Four, reflecting a higher ambient/uncontrolled media share.
